17.1.label: Question 17.1
17.1.scenario: A proprietary long-term insurance company transacts large amounts of individual critical illness and income protection business in a highly competitive market. It is currently reviewing its reinsurance retention limits.

(i).synopsis: Current retention limits (change only if evidence necessary), acceptable fluctuation in experience, size of free assets, size of portfolio, cost of reinsurance vs cost of risk experience fluctuation reserve, level of fluctuation depends on type of business (IP especially volatile), mix of business and variability of benefit sizes, concentration of risk; also consider: guaranteed terms on CI, need for reinsurer assistance (underwriting/claims management especially where company lacks experience), need for financing, cost of reinsurance, future increases to sums insured or sickness benefits, uncertainty around future trends in CI or sickness experience, effect on statutory capital requirements
(i).question: List the factors you would take into account in setting revised limits.

Factors to take into account: Factors to take into account:
• the current retention limits – these should only be changed if there is evidence that this is necessary [½]
• the amount of fluctuation in its experience that the company finds acceptable [½]
• the size of the company's free assets – the lower the acceptable fluctuation, the lower the retention limit [½]
• the size of the portfolio – if small, lower retentions may be needed [½]
• the cost of obtaining reinsurance compared with the cost of financing an appropriate risk experience fluctuation reserve. [½]
The level of fluctuation depends on:
• the type of business – IP experience is especially volatile, ... [½]
... and so the company may be cautious in setting limits for this class [½]
• the mix of business, especially the variability of benefit sizes. [½]
• any concentration of risk (eg geographical or occupational). [½]
The company should also consider:
• the extent to which the insurer is offering guaranteed terms (eg guaranteed or reviewable rates on CI) [½]
• the need for reinsurer's assistance (eg with underwriting or claims management), ... [½]
... especially where the company lacks experience [½]
• the need to obtain financing, which might be obtained through reinsurance [½]
• the cost of reinsurance – the more expensive it is, the more the company will tend to retain [½]
• any future increases to sums insured or sickness benefits under its contracts [½]
• uncertainty around future trends in CI or sickness experience [½]
• the effect of reinsurance on its statutory capital requirements. [½]
[Maximum 7]

(ii).synopsis: Protect actual solvency (especially given uncertainty about future trends in incidence of claims eg impact of screening); protect against solvency; increase capacity to accept risk (write more business with same capital); accept wider range of risks eg different levels of impaired lives; iron out experience fluctuations; reduce new business strain through financing arrangements eg reinsurance commissions; reduce capital requirements; reinsurer advice on underwriting and claims control; expert advice on impaired lives where data scarce; reinsurer software for pricing/underwriting/administering/improving claims handling; reinsurance terms provide check on premium rates; reinsurance may be available at good price (reinsurer accepting loss or tax arbitrage)
(ii).question: State the main reasons why the company might wish to reinsure part of the critical illness insurance business.

Reasons for reinsuring the CI business: It can protect the actual solvency of the company. [½]
This may be especially welcome given uncertainty about future trends in the incidence of claims, eg the impact of screening. [½]
• increase capacity to accept risk, ie you can write more business with the same amount of capital [½]
• allow you to accept very large risks and a wider range of risks, eg different levels of impaired lives. [½]
• iron out experience fluctuations – even for an established insurer, it protects against unwanted 'blips' in the emergence of surplus [½]
• reduce new business strain through financing arrangements, eg reinsurance commissions [½]
• reduce capital requirements, although this is unlikely to be critical unless the company is recently established. [½]
The reinsurer can provide advice on underwriting and claims control. It could also provide temporary support (eg if your underwriter was ill). [½]
Even though the company writes a large amount of this business, it may want expert advice on some classes of impaired lives where data are relatively scarce. [½]
The reinsurer may be able to provide new software to assist with pricing, underwriting, administering or improving the handling of claims. [½]
Such assistance could be particularly important in a competitive market, eg so that the company can price as accurately as possible. [½]
The terms on which the reinsurer will accept business can provide a good check on your premium rates. [½]
The reinsurance may be available at a good price, eg due to:
• the reinsurer accepting the business at a loss (but probably not for long) [½]
• taking advantage of there being a different basis of taxation for reinsurers and ceding offices (tax arbitrage). [½]

(i).synopsis: Means and SDs: A mean 65% sd 25%, B mean 65% sd 25%, C mean 70% sd 12.5%, D mean 60% sd 30%. A and B equally risky (same stop loss risk premium). C higher mean but lower variability; D reverse. Need to consider excess pure premium not just probability of breach. XS 70%: C highest probability of recovery (lowest sd relative to excess), but A/B make recovery at 0.2 sd above mean while 90% is 1 sd above mean so chance of large recovery greater for A or B than C; D recovery at 0.33 sd above mean; C has smallest premium as much less variable; overall result D vs A/B is close call. XS 100%: D greatest chance of any recovery and of large recovery; C least chance; D largest premium, A/B next, C smallest. 25% XS 90%: A/B layer is +1.0 to +2.0 sd; C layer is +1.6 to +3.6 sd; D layer is +1.0 to +1.8 sd; whenever upper limit breached (likeliest D) full recovery of 25% made; premium order same as (b). Illustrative pure premiums (logNormal/Gamma): (a) A,B 7.64%/7.80%, C 4.94%/4.97%, D 7.76%/8.00%; (b) A,B 1.80%/1.55%, C 0.12%/0.08%, D 2.56%/2.25%; (c) A,B 2.09%/2.14%, C 0.48%/0.42%, D 2.21%/2.37%
(i).question:
Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the observed loss ratios for each insurer. On the evidence of these statistics, state, with reasons, which insurer should pay the largest and smallest risk premiums for each of the following stop loss covers in turn:

(a) all losses over 70%

(b) all losses over 100%

(c) losses in the layer 25% excess 90%.

Means and standard deviations: The means and (sample) standard deviations are as follows:
General comments: Looking at these statistics, A and B are equally risky, so should have the same stop loss risk premium. [½]
C has a higher mean loss ratio, but a lower variability. D has the reverse. [½]
When deciding on the risk premiums for stop loss cover, we need to consider the excess pure premium and not just the probability that the limit will be breached. [1]
(a) XS 70%: C will have the highest probability of making a recovery under this contract. [½]
For a symmetrical distribution the chance of exceeding the mean is 50%. For one skew to the right, as is more likely for PMI, the probability is somewhat under 50%. [½]
However, for C to exceed 95%, for example, the result would have to be more than two standard deviations above the mean. [½]
The chance of this for the normal distribution is only about 2½%. [½]
For other distributions the probability may be of a similar order (possibly a little larger). [½]
A or B make a recovery when the result is 0.2 standard deviations above the mean. This is a little less likely than C making a recovery. [½]
However, 90% is only 1 s.d. above the mean, and 2 s.d. above is 115%. This shows that the chance of a large recovery is greater for A or B than for C. [1]
The premium for A or B seems likely to be bigger than that for C (although this 'evidence' is not totally conclusive). [½]
D makes a recovery when the result is 0.33 standard deviations above the mean. This is a little less likely than A or B making a recovery. [1]
However, 90% is only 1 s.d. above the mean, and 2 s.d. above is 120%. This shows that the chance of a large recovery is somewhat greater for D than for A or B. [½]
It seems clear that C will have the smallest premium, because it is much less variable. [½]
The overall result between D and A/B seems to us to be a close call and the result of the calculations may well depend on the assumed distribution of the loss ratio. [1]
(b) XS 100%: D will have the greatest chance of any recovery and also the greatest chance of a large recovery. [1]
C will have the least chance of any recovery and also the least chance of a large recovery. [1]
Hence D should have the largest premium, A and B the next and C has the smallest. [1]
(c) 25% XS 90%: This time we have, in terms of standard deviations above the mean, the layer:
Whenever the upper limit of this layer is breached (likeliest for D) a full recovery of 25% is made. [½]
So it is clear that the premium order must be the same as in (b), ie D should have the largest premium, A and B the next and C has the smallest. [1]
Using a statistical package we get the following illustrative pure premiums by fitting distributions of the stated form with the same moments as the empirical distributions:
Gamma      A,B 1.55%    C 0.08%    D 2.25%    (tail is thinner)

(ii).synopsis: Burning cost pure premiums are average past recoveries if cover had been in place throughout. Results: (a) A 5.8%, B 7.4%, C 4.4%, D 8.0%; (b) A 0.0%, B 1.4%, C 0.0%, D 0.8%; (c) A 0.0%, B 3.4%, C 0.0%, D 2.8%. Dramatically different from (i): B always has much larger premium than A; D not largest for (b) or (c) but B is; A and C each have no premium at all for (b) and (c)
(ii).question: Calculate the historic burning cost pure premium for each insurer and for each of the stop loss covers. Comment on your answers.

Burning cost: The burning costs pure premiums are just the average past recoveries, if the cover had been in place throughout. These are as follows:
These are dramatically different from the results in part (i). Now:
• B always has a much larger premium than A [1 each]
• D is not the largest for (b) or (c), but B is [1 each]
• A and C each have no premium at all for (b) and (c). [1 each]

(iii).synopsis: Neither (i) nor (ii) alone is suitable. (i) penalises A relative to B even though B makes large losses when it does claim (exactly what high-layer stop loss protects against). (ii) gives zero premium for A and C for (b) and (c) despite some chance of future recovery. Ideal: determine prospective distribution of loss ratio and calculate pure premium for any layer directly from that distribution; determine this by studying all relevant historical information for the risk and others that shed light; adjust carefully for changes in factors including: precise cover provided (if reducing, future loss amounts not as bad as past ones), effect of inflation (measure correctly accounting for mix of past and next year's prospective claims), insurance cycle (eg premiums rising 20% reduces loss ratios by factor 100/120). Practical approach: do as much as possible analytically, then look at results on various bases (including (i) and (ii)), then pass results to highly skilled health underwriter to estimate suitable risk premium taking analytical results as part of their deliberations
(iii).question: Outline how you could rate a stop loss treaty.

Rating a stop loss treaty: Clearly neither (i) nor (ii), by itself, provides a suitable way to rate a stop loss treaty. [½]
In particular (i) penalises A relative to B, although past experience suggests that when B makes a loss at all it makes a relatively big loss, ... [1]
... which is exactly what unlimited or high layer stop loss cover is protecting against. [½]
In (ii) it seems inappropriate to have a zero pure premium for cover (b) or (c) to A or C, despite there (presumably) being some chance that a recovery would be made in the future. [1]
The ideal would be to decide, somehow, what the prospective distribution of the loss ratio for any company is. [½]
We could then calculate the pure premium for any layer of cover directly from that distribution. [½]
Whether it is an analytical or empirical one, we could do this given a suitable computer package or a very large envelope to write on the back of. [½]
So, then, we just need to decide what the distribution of next year's loss ratios is. [½]
To make as good a job as possible of this, we would study all relevant historical information for the risk, and also all others that could shed some light on the distribution for this risk. [½]
We would need to be careful about the effects of changes in lots of factors, including:
• the precise cover provided, if it's reducing then future loss amounts will not be as bad as past ones [1]
• the effect of inflation and, in particular, the measure which correctly accounts for the mix of past claims and next year's prospective claims from this portfolio [1]
• the insurance cycle, eg if premiums will rise next year by 20% but other things are equal, then loss ratios next year will reduce by a factor of 100/120. [1]
The ideal practical approach would probably be to do as much as possible analytically. [½]

Then look at the result on various bases (including (i) and (ii)), then pass those results to a highly skilled health underwriter for them to estimate a suitable risk premium, taking the analytical results as part of their deliberations. [1]
